section.title: To Live Is Christ
section.sectionRationale: Paul turns from prayer to his own imprisonment and shows how God has used even chains to advance the gospel. Whether preachers act from goodwill or rivalry, Christ is proclaimed and Paul rejoices. The section climbs to the letter's most famous confession, to live is Christ and to die is gain, where Paul weighs death against continued service and chooses whatever most magnifies Christ and helps the church.
section.thesis: Paul's imprisonment has served to advance the gospel and embolden others to preach, so that whether by false motive or true Christ is proclaimed and Paul rejoices; and because for him to live is Christ and to die is gain, he faces death without fear, longing to be with Christ yet content to remain for the sake of the Philippians' progress and joy in the faith.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the gospel advancing through Paul's chains; his joy that Christ is preached whatever the motive; and the confession that to live is Christ and to die is gain, resolved in staying for the church's sake.

verses.text: Now I want you to know, brothers, that my circumstances have actually served to {{prokope|advance}} the gospel.
As a result, it has become clear throughout the whole {{praitorion|palace guard}} and to everyone else that I am in chains for Christ.
And most of the brothers, confident in the Lord by my chains, now dare more greatly to speak the word without fear.

units.label: Christ preached, and Paul rejoices
units.note: whether by false motive or true

verses.text: It is true that some preach Christ out of envy and rivalry, but others out of goodwill.
The latter do so in love, knowing that I am appointed for the defense of the gospel.
The former, however, preach Christ out of selfish ambition, not sincerely, supposing that they can add to the distress of my chains.
What then is the issue? Just this: that in every way, whether by false motives or true, Christ is preached. And in this I rejoice. Yes, and I will continue to rejoice,
because I know that through your prayers and the provision of the Spirit of Jesus Christ, my distress will turn out for my deliverance.
I eagerly expect and hope that I will in no way be ashamed, but will have complete boldness so that now as always Christ will be {{megalyno|exalted}} in my body, whether by life or by death.

units.label: To live is Christ, to die is gain
units.note: torn between two, staying for their sake

verses.text: For to me, to live is Christ, and to die is {{kerdos|gain}}.
But if I go on living in the body, this will mean fruitful labor for me. So what shall I choose? I do not know.
I am torn between the two. I desire to {{analyo|depart}} and be with Christ, which is far better indeed.
But it is more necessary for you that I remain in the body.
Convinced of this, I know that I will remain and will continue with all of you for your progress and joy in the faith,
so that through my coming to you again your exultation in Christ Jesus will resound on account of me.

prokope.gloss: Advance, progress, a going forward, a word used of an army cutting a path ahead. Paul says his imprisonment has not hindered but advanced the gospel; what looked like a setback became a road for the good news to press forward into the palace guard and beyond.

praitorion.gloss: The Praetorium, the imperial guard or governor's headquarters. Paul's chains put him in daily contact with elite Roman soldiers, and through them the reason for his imprisonment, Christ, became known throughout the guard. The gospel reached the very heart of Roman power through a prisoner.

megalyno.gloss: To magnify, to make great, to exalt. Paul's single ambition is that Christ be magnified in his body, whether he lives or dies. His life is not about his own vindication but about displaying the greatness of Christ, in freedom or in death, with complete boldness.

kerdos.gloss: Gain, profit, advantage. To die is gain because death ushers the believer into the immediate presence of Christ, which Paul calls far better. Death is not loss for the Christian but the doorway to the greatest possible profit, being with the Lord he loves.

analyo.gloss: To depart, to loosen, as of a ship casting off its moorings or a soldier striking camp. Paul pictures death not as extinction but as departure, a setting sail to be with Christ. The word gives death the character of a journey home rather than an end.

basic.context: Paul writes from prison, and the Philippians are worried for him. He reassures them: far from silencing him, his chains have pushed the gospel forward. The palace guard now knows why he is imprisoned, and other believers are speaking more boldly. Some preach from bad motives, some from good, but Christ is preached, and that is enough to make Paul rejoice. Then he opens his heart: living means Christ, dying means gain, and he will gladly stay to help this church grow.
basic.hermeneutics: This is autobiography with a purpose. Paul turns his own hard circumstances into an example, teaching the Philippians how to face suffering, rivalry, and even death with joy centered on Christ. The famous line of verse 21 is not a slogan but the settled conviction of a man in chains.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul's chains led to the prokope, the advance, of the gospel, even into the praitorion, the palace guard. His one aim is that Christ be megalyno, magnified, in his body by life or death. To die is kerdos, gain, and he longs to analyo, to depart, like a ship casting off, and be with Christ.
basic.typology: Paul's willingness to be poured out for the gospel and to count death as gain follows the pattern of Christ Himself, who through death won life for many. The suffering servant who does not shrink from shame but trusts God to vindicate him is echoed in Paul's confident hope that he will not be put to shame.
basic.christ: Christ is everything here: the content of all true preaching, the one to be magnified in Paul's body, the whole meaning of Paul's living, and the joy that makes his dying gain. To depart is to be with Christ, which is far better than anything this life offers. Christ is both the reason to live and the reward of death.
basic.god: God is at work behind the scenes, turning imprisonment into gospel advance and even mixed motives into the spread of Christ's name. The Spirit of Jesus Christ supplies Paul in his distress. God is sovereign over Paul's chains and over his life and death, and works all of it for His glory and the church's good.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 8:28 says God works all things for good. 2 Corinthians 5:8 speaks of being away from the body and at home with the Lord. Acts 16 tells of the founding of the Philippian church in the shadow of a prison.
basic.oneStory: God has always advanced His purposes through the suffering of His servants, and supremely through the death of His Son. Here Paul joins that story: his chains carry the gospel forward, and his readiness to die for Christ points to the hope of resurrection life secured by Christ's own death and rising.

context.literary: The section moves from report, verses 12 to 18, to personal reflection, verses 19 to 26, held together by the repeated note of joy and the theme of the gospel's advance whatever the cost. Verse 21 is the pivot, a terse two part confession that governs the weighing of life and death that follows.
context.historical: Paul writes as a prisoner awaiting trial, most likely in Rome, guarded by soldiers of the imperial Praetorium. Rival preachers, perhaps jealous of Paul's influence or resentful of his imprisonment, use his confinement as a chance to promote themselves. The Philippians, who had sent aid, feared for his life and his mission.
context.canonical: The passage joins a biblical chorus on suffering that serves God's purposes, from Joseph to the prophets to the cross, and on the believer's hope beyond death, which the New Testament grounds in Christ's resurrection. Verse 21 stands among Scripture's clearest statements that death for the Christian is entrance into Christ's presence.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reassure the anxious Philippians that Paul's imprisonment has advanced rather than hindered the gospel, to model joy in Christ amid suffering and rivalry, and to show a Christ centered freedom that makes both life and death gain, resolving in a commitment to serve them further.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul describes his particular situation, chains, rival preachers, an uncertain verdict, but the underlying convictions are meant to be shared: rejoice that Christ is preached, live to magnify Him, and face death as gain. The command implied throughout is to adopt Paul's Christ centered outlook.

debates.question: Should Paul rejoice that rivals preach Christ from bad motives?

views.label: He rejoices that Christ is preached, not in their sin
views.case: Paul distinguishes the message from the motive; the true gospel is going out, and he rejoices in that, while still naming the envy and selfish ambition as wrong.

views.label: He commends their motives
views.case: This misreads the text; Paul plainly calls their motive envy, rivalry, and selfish ambition, and rejoices only that Christ is nonetheless proclaimed.

debates.bearing: Paul's joy is anchored in the spread of Christ's name, not in approving of impure motives; the gospel is bigger than its flawed messengers.

debates.question: What does Paul expect at death, and what is the state of believers who die?

views.label: Immediate presence with Christ
views.case: To depart is to be with Christ, which is far better; Paul expects conscious fellowship with the Lord at death, awaiting the resurrection of the body.

views.label: Only a future resurrection with no interim
views.case: Some stress the resurrection so strongly they downplay the interim; yet Paul's far better presses for a real gain at death itself, being with Christ.

debates.bearing: The verse gives comfort that death brings the believer into Christ's presence, the intermediate joy that precedes the resurrection of the body.

study.originalLanguages: Paul reframes his imprisonment with prokope, advance or progress, a term of forward movement: the gospel has cut a path ahead through his chains, reaching even the praitorion, the imperial guard. His governing ambition is that Christ be megalyno, magnified or made great, in his body whether by life or death. The confession of verse 21 sets to live is Christ against to die is kerdos, gain, sheer profit, because death brings him to the Lord. He describes dying with analyo, to loose or cast off, the image of a ship leaving harbor or a tent being struck, so that death becomes departure to be with Christ, which he calls far better.

typology.type: The servant not put to shame
typology.fulfillment: Paul's confident hope that he will not be ashamed echoes the trusting servant of Isaiah, ultimately fulfilled in Christ who endured shame and was vindicated.
Paul can call death gain only because Christ's death and resurrection have turned the grave into the doorway to the Lord's presence and the hope of resurrection.

study.christ: Christ is the sum of Paul's existence. He is the one preached whatever the motive, the one to be magnified in Paul's living and dying, the meaning captured in to live is Christ, and the gain of death, for to depart is to be with Him, which is far better. Paul's freedom in the face of execution flows entirely from his union with Christ: he cannot lose, because life gives him Christ to serve and death gives him Christ to enjoy.
study.god: God rules over Paul's chains, turning them into gospel advance, and even weaves impure motives into the spread of His Son's name. The Spirit of Jesus Christ supplies Paul in his distress, and God will see to it that Christ is exalted in Paul whether he lives or dies. Here is a sovereign, gracious God who wastes no suffering and secures His servant's ultimate good.

crossReferences.note: To be away from the body is to be at home with the Lord, matching depart and be with Christ.
God works all things together for good, the logic behind chains that advance the gospel.
The founding of the Philippian church, itself born amid imprisonment and gospel advance.
I no longer live, but Christ lives in me, the root of to live is Christ.

study.oneStory: Throughout Scripture God advances His saving purpose through the suffering of His servants and supremely through the death of His Son. Paul stands in that line: his imprisonment carries the gospel into Caesar's household, and his readiness to die rests on the hope Christ won by rising. What looks like defeat, chains and the threat of execution, becomes another chapter in the triumph of the gospel.

quiz.q: What effect did Paul's imprisonment actually have on the gospel?

options.text: It advanced the gospel, making Christ known even in the palace guard.
options.note: Verses 12 to 13 say his chains served to advance the gospel.

options.text: It brought the gospel to a standstill.
options.note: The opposite: Paul says his circumstances advanced the gospel.

options.text: It made the other believers too afraid to speak.
options.note: Verse 14 says most brothers grew bolder, not more afraid.

options.text: It caused Paul to abandon his faith.
options.note: Paul's faith and joy only deepened in prison.

quiz.q: Why can Paul rejoice even when some preach Christ from bad motives?

options.text: Because Christ is still being proclaimed, whatever the motive.
options.note: Verse 18: whether by false motives or true, Christ is preached, and Paul rejoices.

options.text: Because he thinks their envy is admirable.
options.note: He rejoices that Christ is preached, not in their envy or ambition.

options.text: Because their preaching does not really matter.
options.note: It matters greatly; that is why the spread of Christ brings him joy.

options.text: Because he enjoys competition for its own sake.
options.note: His joy is in Christ being preached, not in rivalry.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by to live is Christ and to die is gain?

options.text: Living means serving and knowing Christ, and dying means being with Him, which is better still.
options.note: Verses 21 to 23 hold living for Christ and dying to be with Him together as gain.

options.text: That life is meaningless and only death matters.
options.note: Paul values remaining for fruitful labor; life for Christ is deeply meaningful.

options.text: That Christians should seek death quickly.
options.note: Paul chooses to remain for the church's sake; he does not seek to hasten death.

options.text: That death is simply the end of everything.
options.note: Death is gain because it means being with Christ, not extinction.

quiz.q: Why does Paul expect to remain rather than depart?

options.text: Because it is more necessary for the Philippians' progress and joy in the faith.
options.note: Verses 24 to 25 have Paul stay for the church's good.

options.text: Because he is afraid to die.
options.note: He is not afraid; he even prefers to depart and be with Christ.

options.text: Because he doubts there is life after death.
options.note: He is sure death means being with Christ, far better.

options.text: Because he wants to avoid all suffering.
options.note: He stays to serve, not to escape hardship.

quiz.q: What is Paul's single ambition in life and death?

options.text: That Christ would be magnified in his body, whether by life or death.
options.note: Verse 20 states his aim, that Christ be exalted in him either way.

options.text: That he would be released and honored.
options.note: His concern is Christ's honor, not his own release or reputation.

options.text: That his rivals would be silenced.
options.note: He rejoices that Christ is preached even by rivals.

options.text: That he would be remembered as a great man.
options.note: He wants Christ, not himself, to be magnified.

questions: Paul saw his imprisonment as a way the gospel could advance. Where might God be using a hard circumstance in your life to move the gospel forward?
Paul rejoiced that Christ was preached even by rivals. What would it take for you to care more about Christ being known than about who gets the credit?
For Paul, to live is Christ. If you finished that sentence honestly, to live is what, what would you say, and what would you want to change?
Paul faced death as gain because it meant being with Christ. How does that hope change the way you think about your own death?
Paul chose to stay for the good of others rather than his own comfort. Whose progress and joy in the faith is God calling you to serve?

translationNotes.note: Palace guard renders praitorion; a footnote in the BSB notes the alternative all the Praetorium, the imperial guard or governor's headquarters.
Deliverance translates soteria, which can mean rescue or salvation; Paul echoes Job 13:16 and may intend both his vindication and his final salvation.
Depart renders analyo, to loose or cast off, the image of a ship leaving harbor or a tent being struck, picturing death as departure to be with Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Philippians 1:12-26 this session. No em or en dashes occur in this passage.
